speaker: interviewer
text: --March, 1996. Fira, the river Bug--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they made that a border.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did that impact on you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it-- I didn't-- you know, it didn't bother us. It, it bothered us that the Russians had to leave, and we awaited the Germans. So it took two weeks. The Germans arrived to-- I wrote it down-- the Germans arrived on the 9th of October in the morning. Right away somehow, they-- it did-- nothing happened. But people start to be terrified. And the place where I lived, the owner had-- they were-- they had a mill, a flour mill--

speaker: interviewer
text: A flour mill, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Chelm. And also they had in, in Lutsk. So they knew, most probably. And they packed up a few, things, and they left on the-- on that-- together when the Russians were still there. [SIGHS] And so when the Germans arrived, that was stationed five officers above us in this place. So this five officer-- I-- I was so terrified [LAUGHS] of them. And when the Germans came in, they started to catch young people. And they, they took them to, to clean the Kasernes and-- in a funny way, we sold clothes. They asked to take-- people were telling us these things. That's why I didn't go. I was sitting home. And so they that-- that's what-- they took people. They shaved their beards. And so we, we heard the stories, and we were really very terrified.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was still-- that was the army, which wasn't so bad. On on the 20th-- I think that was the day I left, the 20th of September-- the 20th of November. So I was six weeks still in Chelm. And at that time, my brother-in-law left before this. He left together with the Red Army. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meilech. He-- and a lot of young people left because they-- there was propaganda that the Germans are taking young men and sending to the French front, which I don't-- that [LAUGHS] couldn't have been. And so my brother-in-law left. And then my sister came. And it took-- a lot of people traveled back and forth. It wasn't so well established, the border. [SIGHS] And then my sister with a little baby, she-- he was only seven months old, came. And my father told her to follow her husband. He sent my brother with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bayer. And people still could come back and send letter-- I mean, letters by-- not by post, too. And a lot of people left. And the last post, it was a Saturday. And the Russians, they took everything. And they suggested-- they could-- they gave you know, the train and, and, and the tracks. And then you could load everything and, and leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: But my, my mother decided we will all go. And we packed in, in sacks a lot of things. But my father, when he opened the wardrobe, and he said, you see? That's what I worked for so many years. And now-- because it was-- it wasn't-- people was on the other-- across the border, they'd been sleeping on stations, in schools. And so-- and my father knew about it. He said, where will we go, to leave everything and to start all anew? My mother was very energetic. And she was so angry. She said, you are already, you know, climbing that-- and she took it out from the sacks and packed, packed the things. That's what happened. And so my brother, when he was there-- and after our-- after, when six weeks, I was with the Germans, and my brother-in-law came back. [SIGHS] When he came back to pick-- to ask me to come with him, and my mother, you know, they, they already did two children she had there. And she didn't want to. She said, how could we be parted?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I wanted to go. But I didn't-- I didn't even think-- it's funny, that sometimes I, I have such a-- a, a feeling. I feel guilty that for me to, to go, to part from family-- because everybody thought it's only for a time being. For me, it was-- it was like an adventure. I never been away from home. I never been anywhere. So it was, for me, a big thing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] anyway, he persuade my mother. He promised he will be a father and mother to me. And they let me go. And that was the day the SS arrived. And we have to cross the [NON-ENGLISH] on the-- to the outskirts of town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a forest already. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: How, how old were you, Fira, when that-- when--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 17, [CRYING] but a young 17, very naive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we had to hide from, from one house to another so that we wouldn't be caught, because they start already to, to catch people. And when we came on the outskirts, we sat-- I-- in this wagon. He hired a-- how would you say, a cart?

speaker: interviewer
text: A horse and cart?

speaker: interviewee
text: A horse and cart. And we had this-- a bit of-- a few things, which he took it-- my brother-in-law took it from his home. [SIGHS] And that was beginning from the forests. And this I always-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I remember it, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I say goodbye to my youngest sister. [SOBBING] She screamed, she said, take me with you! [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But I, I-- somehow, I was so-- I was happy that I am going. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then my parents left. The street was-- [SOBBING] I turned and I saw-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So that night, we came to our-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to our village near the Bug. Do you remember the name of the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I don't. It's such a, you know, [INAUDIBLE] little village. And we-- and my brother-in-law, he left me there in a-- like, in a-- in a-- like, not a pub. It's just like a [NON-ENGLISH]. And he went out to find some of the fishermen to, to take us across the border. He came back. And he said, when-- as soon, it will-- it was such a-- such a dark night that you could cut with a knife, so, so dark it was. And this fisherman took us-- we had all things on our back and very low. And he took us on this. And he told us to, as soon we come across, to take our things to go as far away from, from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what it was, the-- on that side, the village wasn't near the water, near the river. But on the other side was a step, was nothing. The, the-- it start. The village was a forest and was a steppe, nothing there. So he said that the Russian guard are, are guarding the, the river-- to go away as far as we could. So we came across. And we started from one, a few steps, carried away the things. And it was like, like, you know, a lower [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ground. And we put our things, and we were sitting there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my brother-in-law, on the way coming back to Chelm, he had also a-- such a fisherman on the other side. And he made an appointment with him that after two days, at that-and-that day, he will wait for him. And he thought he will wait for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he left me there. And he went to look for him. In the meantime, what happened? They-- the Russian guard caught him. And they brought him in to a-- it was such a, a place where, that night, a lot of people crossed border, you know, in different places. And, and what the Russians did, whatever-- whomever they caught, they sent it back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and I was lying there, waiting for my brother-in-law all night. And the whole night, it was shooting. And I-- and, and, you know, most probably, the guards were guarding the, the border. And it was shooting. And I was lying there. And my brother-in-law didn't come. I didn't know what's happened. I thought it's only one thing-- he couldn't find the place because it was a field, a steppe, nothing. And in the morning, the sun start to, to get up. I lift myself, and I notice on the-- near the water, the Russian guard, two of them, marching, guarding. So I was afraid. So I put myself back in. And funny, it-- for me, I think, it was-- it was a miracle because it was like a, a shrub growing. And here I was lying. And I saw their boots. They passed by me. And they-- and they didn't see me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they were far away, I, I thought, I don't know what happened. What should I do? I stood up, And I thought, whatever will happen. And I saw a-- a farmer came out with a wife and a horse and carriage, far, far away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, I, I will run to him and ask him, maybe he can take me to where I want to go. I didn't know what's happened. What's happened that-- since my brother-in-law left, they made that nobody is allowed to go near the border. And I start to run to this farmer. And I came there. I start to cry. And I tell-- I told him a story that my parents got killed. He knew in Chelm was a big bombing, a big fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I find out that I have a brother here. And I am going to my brother. Could he take my things and bring me? Because the next little town was Lubon, Lubon.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so he said, look, before we could do such a thing. But already for a few days, we are not allowed. If they catch somebody near the border, they send to Siberia. So he didn't want to.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, what should I do? Then he noticed, he said, you know this, they call [NON-ENGLISH], the big-- the, the officer or somebody, is on a horse-- went on a-- for a ride. So he took off the hat and started to wave to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he came. And I, I said, I can't speak Russian. But you tell him, because that was a Ukrainian. I said, you tell him what I told you. And he came back. He was sitting on a horse. And I was standing there, watching and crying. And he told him the story, what's happened. When I told them that I, I was a whole night there-- so the farmer's wife crossed herself. That-- then I discovered it was so dangerous there. And he told him-- the the Russian officer told the farmer, all right, go and pick up her things and bring them. I thought that he's going to take me to my sister. [LAUGHS] And when I arrive-- when I-- he went with me. I could-- you know, everywhere looked the same thing. So I took time till I found my, my things. And he brought me into this point where it was-- maybe hundreds of people. And then came out. And my brother-in-law, he saw me. He thought that he is going to die.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? They took him-- us-- that he crossed the border. And came out, this officer. He had the list who goes back. Who came from-- want to cross the border, they didn't let, and who crossed the border, they want to send it back. So he was on the list, they want to send him back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I, I-- when I-- he came, this officer. And I went around, and I said, that's my brother. And because he knew that I am going. So he said, you have brave sister. Take her and go home. And that's when we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, It was so many-- the conditions, we were living--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she? What town?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Lubon, yes. You see, they settled-- everybody tried to settle close because we thought that wouldn't take long. We will go back home. So we didn't travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were-- we were there already, my brother and my sister, my brother-in-law, the baby, and another two or three families sleeping. We made such a-- benches and straw on, on it. And, and then my brother-- we saw it's already winter. It's-- it was the conditions. We want to go to a bigger city.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when we went to Lutsk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To travel on a train wasn't so easy. Anyway, we arrived in Lutsk. And we did-- and my brother-in-law found a, a uncle. And he had six children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they start to go to look for a place where to live. Anyway, they found a, a-- it was two rooms, which was empty. The owner prepared this-- his sister was supposed to get married. And that's where she want.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they went in just opened the door. They broke in. And so he couldn't throw them out. And we were about, about 15 [LAUGHS] people together sleeping on the floor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there, we were not, not long. We-- you know, nobody worked. We ran out of money. And so came from Russia-- a Russian from Moscow, they were sent to organize people, to get them to Russia for work.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother-in-law went there. It was maybe, maybe 30 of them. It was a long table, each Russian, whoever you went in-- he registered. And we went-- later, we left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Each, each one, each-- this Russian-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they belong to the party. And so each one has his own wagon. And each wagon went to a different city. That's where I was with Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH]. We joined-- we, we didn't know each other. That's how we traveled.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They told us, but we didn't know. They told us Taganrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did you know what sort of work, or for what reason you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- did you stay with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us we, we-- that's they are bringing-- look, when you don't know the language-- they told her we are going to Taganrog. And it is a sea there. That is the Azov Sea, which joined to the Black Sea. And we are going to work on a [POLISH]. [POLISH]-- in Poland-- in Polish, [POLISH] is a-- is a basket. So we-- so we laughed. We said most probably that the sea [INAUDIBLE] the thing we will [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS] And so we didn't know. And we arrived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, when was that? When did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in the-- January--

speaker: interviewee
text: January, 1940. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you been corresponding with your parents in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it was, the day when I left Chelm-- 11 days later on the 1st of-- on the 1st of December was the first pogrom. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was-- they collected the, the-- from the welfare-- the president from the welfare went from home to home. And they had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] only men from 16 to 60-- had together on, on a plaza there. And apparently, when-- it was about 700 of them. And they been already-- so the Germans were on bicycles, on, on horses, on, on motorbikes and dogs and had them all around. And they told them they are going to, to march to the Russian border to the Bug. So at that time, a lot of-- a lot of got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father amongst--?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father, I-- we met, still, in Lubon-- we met one, which knew my father, which survived. He swam through there. Could you imagine in Poland in December that-- to swim in the river? [SIGHS] It was-- so he told us that my father was in it, too. And we didn't know if he's-- was killed because on the way, whoever-- you know, it was quite a lot of kilometers to make. Who was weak and who couldn't, so they shoot them on the spot. And the rest, when-- was when they brought them to the Bug, they told them to swim. And a lot which got over, the Russians caught them. They sent them back. Anyway, we didn't know nothing about what happened to our father. But being already in Taganrog, we had a letter that my father was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he had a bullet in his leg. And he was-- he was injured. And he fell down. He played that he's dead. And when the whole party passed, he crawled to a farmer. And the farmer came and-- later to Chelm and told my mother. And so I don't know what happened. My mother must probably brought him back because none of the letters we ever had. We knew that father is sick because she-- in letters, my mother used to say which doctor is coming to treat him. And most probably-- that what I think, that he died from this wound. And you know, in my yizkor book, it's all written about this march. And there, there, it was already the 1st of December. And a lot of-- a lot of-- didn't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Fira, what happened to you in Taganrog? What, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Taganrog, they brought us. And they-- and they treated us very nice, which was really-- there was such a respect because we were really different than the Russian. And they gave us work, even people which nobody-- they didn't give people by profession, only where they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my, my brother-in-law-- that was the [RUSSIAN]. In, in Russian, that's leather-- a, a tannery. And it was a tannery which belonged to the government. It was working about 30,000 people.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- my brother-in-law was sent to the lowest. It was the dirtiest thing. My brother was sent to where already the hard leather was. But as you see from photos, my brother is such a delicate person. And they, they-- he was-- they measure and make-- made the balls of, of this leather.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his job was to carry from-- to, to the storeroom. So he couldn't-- [LAUGHS] so he was standing-- that the Russians, they were very healthy, strong people. So they used to take him on top of it and carry it to the-- [LAUGHS] And I was sent to, to the last process, where the fine, very fine, leather was made. And they put me next to a machine which, when I looked at it, it frightened me. And a man was supposed to teach me to operate this machine. That was the polishing machine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came for two weeks dressed up, because, you know, from home, you take the best clothes. And I was standing there. He couldn't explain to me in-- because he-- Russian. And I, I couldn't understand him. I couldn't ask. And only by watching I had to learn this-- how this machine operate.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, after two weeks, they told-- they put him on a bigger job. And I-- they put me there. Anyway, I learned. I learned. And I was feeling out the norm because it was there they called [NON-ENGLISH]. What more I do over the norm, I got bigger wages. The money wasn't nothing worth. And then when the-- it start the-- at that time was only the fine leather they had. After, when the war-- German-Russian war broke out, they introduced the heavy, heavy leather for, for the army. And there, they, they used in the laboratory-- mix the pint with blood. And this came rats. And I'm afraid for a mouse. So could you imagine rats, like, like, so-- such a big ones, I never saw anything. And they wasn't afraid. I was-- then they put us to work, two shifts only, from 7:00 to 7:00, from 7:00 to 7:00 morning. So every week, so it was 14 hours a day. And when I had to go to work on this night shift, I thought I, I will die. You know, it-- the lamp over this, this machine was hanging low. And a rat was standing-- the eyes glitter. And I, I was so terrified. Anyway, that was till, till the day came. And we-- they made a meeting, and they told us that the front is close, and whoever could, to, to be evacuated to, to run. We knew about it, so we always had packed a rucksack so that wouldn't-- because the city Taganrog is surrounded with the sea. It's only one highway to go-- which goes to Rostov. And that's when we-- he-- and pointed out a few of us Polish people. Because they said we were so far away from home. You better leave as you could.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop here.
